title: EdgeWorkers
subtitle: JavaScript at the edge — sub-ms cold start

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: EdgeWorkers are receptionists at every Akamai office worldwide. They handle paperwork (auth, redirects, A/B routing) before bothering head office (your origin).

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
EdgeWorkers run JavaScript at every Akamai PoP — 4,000+ globally. They hook into the request lifecycle:

- onClientRequest — before cache lookup
- onOriginRequest — before forwarding to origin
- onOriginResponse / onClientResponse — modify replies

Sub-millisecond cold start. CPU-time bounded. Pair with EdgeKV for global low-latency state.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Common workloads: JWT validation, geo-routing, A/B test variant selection, header rewriting, image transforms, valet-claim-check token patterns. For longer-running or Wasm workloads, use Akamai Functions (Fermyon Spin) on Linode instead.
"EdgeWorkers run JS at every PoP. Use them to make decisions before traffic ever hits your origin."

quiz.question: Where does an EdgeWorker actually execute?

options.explain: EdgeWorkers run at every PoP — that's the point.

options.text: At the Akamai PoP closest to the user
options.explain: JavaScript runs at the edge — 4,000+ PoPs — for sub-ms latency.
That's a Service Worker, not an EdgeWorker.
EdgeWorkers are Akamai's native runtime, not Lambda@Edge.

quiz.question: When should you reach for Akamai Functions (Spin) instead of EdgeWorkers?

options.text: When you need lower CPU-time limits
options.explain: Functions actually allow longer execution and richer runtimes.

options.text: When you need Wasm, longer execution, or Rust/Go/TS
options.explain: Functions = Fermyon Spin on Linode. Wasm-based, supports multiple languages, longer execution windows.
EdgeWorkers are JS-only; Functions support more.
Read-only isn't the deciding factor — language and execution model are.
